title: Hooked
subtitle: How to Build Habit-Forming Products
author: Nir Eyal
tagline: The four-step loop behind every product you reach for without thinking.
whyRead: Read it twice. As a builder, it hands you a repeatable engine — Trigger, Action, Variable Reward, Investment — for turning a product people try into one they can't stop using, plus the ethics test to keep you honest. As a user, it is X-ray glasses: once you see the four steps running on your own attention, the pull of the feed, the badge, and the notification stops feeling like an accident and starts looking like design you can push back on.

map.intro:
Some products don't just get used — they get used automatically, cued by the smallest prompt, before conscious thought has a say. Nir Eyal calls the engine behind them the **Hook Model**: a four-phase loop companies run again and again until behavior hardens into habit.

The loop has four parts. A **Trigger** cues the behavior — at first an external prompt (a notification, an ad, a friend's message), later an internal one, when an emotion like boredom or loneliness fires the urge on its own. The **Action** is the simplest possible behavior done in anticipation of a reward. The **Variable Reward** satisfies the user while leaving them wanting more — unpredictability is what keeps attention locked in. The **Investment** is work the user puts in (data, content, followers, effort) that both improves the product and loads the next trigger.

Each trip through the loop makes the next one likelier. Preferences get shaped, value gets stored, and the external trigger fades as the internal one takes the wheel.

Eyal's larger claim is that habits, not features, decide which products win. Habit-forming products enjoy higher customer lifetime value, more pricing freedom, faster word-of-mouth growth, and a moat rivals can't cross by copying features alone. This is a builder's manual — and, read the other way, a field guide to why your own attention keeps getting captured, and how to notice when it happens.
map.howToUse: Start with Chapter 1 to decide whether a habit strategy even fits what you're building. Then move through Trigger, Action, Variable Reward, and Investment in order — each phase sets up the next. Treat Chapter 6 as a mandatory ethics gut-check before you ship. Chapter 7 shows you how to test for real habits and spot fresh hook opportunities.

chapters.title: The Habit Zone
chapters.summary:
A **habit** is a behavior done with little or no conscious thought, and Eyal argues that products which create them dominate their markets. Habits pay off in four ways: higher **customer lifetime value** (users stay and spend longer), more **pricing flexibility** (loyal users tolerate price changes), stronger **growth** (frequent users invite others), and a real **competitive edge** — a habit is a defensive moat that switching to a merely-better rival can't overcome.

He separates **vitamins** (nice-to-have, appeal to emotion) from **painkillers** (solve an obvious pain). Habit-forming products often start as vitamins, then become painkillers: once the habit forms, its absence causes an itch that the product relieves. That itch is the hook.

Whether a habit is even possible depends on the **habit zone** — the intersection of **frequency** (how often the behavior occurs) and **perceived utility** (how useful it seems versus alternatives). Behaviors done often enough can become habits even at modest utility (Google beats a marginally-better Bing because it's already the reflex); rare behaviors need very high utility. If a behavior can't be made frequent, a habit strategy won't stick.
readIf: You're deciding whether your product even needs to be habit-forming
You want to know why loyal users don't switch to "better" competitors
You keep hearing "vitamin vs. painkiller" and want the real distinction

chapters.title: Trigger: What Sparks the Behavior
chapters.summary:
Every habit starts with a **trigger**, and Eyal sorts them into external and internal. **External triggers** carry the instruction of what to do next inside them and come in four types: **paid** (advertising), **earned** (press, viral mentions, app-store features), **relationship** (one person telling or inviting another), and **owned** (real estate the user has agreed to let you occupy — an app icon, an email subscription, a notification). Owned and relationship triggers drive repeat engagement; paid and earned mostly acquire.

The real prize is the **internal trigger**: when the product becomes tied to a thought, routine, or — most powerfully — an emotion. Negative emotions are potent fuel; **boredom, loneliness, frustration, indecision, and FOMO** send people reaching for a product to make the discomfort go away. We check Instagram when bored, Google when uncertain, Facebook when lonely, often within seconds and without deciding to.

To find your product's internal trigger, Eyal borrows Toyota's **5 Whys**: name a user, name a behavior, and ask "why" five times until you reach the underlying emotion the product soothes.
readIf: You're spending on ads but users don't come back on their own
You want your product to become an automatic reflex, not a reminder
You want to see which emotion sends you to your phone

chapters.title: Action: Making the Behavior Easy
chapters.summary:
For a behavior to happen, three things must converge at once, captured in **B = MAT** (Behavior = Motivation, Ability, Trigger, from BJ Fogg). If any one is missing — enough motivation, enough ability, a present trigger — the action fails.

Because boosting motivation is slow and expensive, Eyal focuses on **ability**: making the action radically simpler. Simplicity is a function of the scarcest of six resources — **time, money, physical effort, brain cycles, social deviance, and non-routineness**. Remove whichever one the user has least of. Facebook's one-click login and Google's single search box win by demanding almost nothing.

Motivation still matters, and Fogg names three core motivator pairs: **seek pleasure / avoid pain, seek hope / avoid fear, seek social acceptance / avoid rejection**.

Eyal then adds cognitive **heuristics** that lift action without adding features: **scarcity** (Worchel's cookies were rated more desirable when only two sat in the jar), **framing** (the same wine tastes better when labeled expensive), **anchoring**, and the **endowed progress effect** (a loyalty card pre-stamped with "free" progress gets completed far more often).
readIf: Users understand your product but still don't take the first step
You're tempted to add features when you should be removing friction
You want to spot the tricks that make one more tap feel effortless

chapters.title: Variable Reward: The Itch You Can't Scratch
chapters.summary:
Rewards keep users coming back, but **variable** rewards keep them hooked. The brain's craving runs on anticipation: Olds and Milner's 1954 rats pressed a lever wired to their pleasure center until they collapsed, and Stanford's Brian Knutson later showed with fMRI that the nucleus accumbens lights up in *anticipation* of a reward, not on receiving it. Unpredictability is the engine.

Eyal names three reward classes. **Rewards of the Tribe** are social — acceptance, importance, inclusion (Facebook likes, Stack Overflow points). **Rewards of the Hunt** are the chase for resources or information (slot machines, the endless scroll of a feed). **Rewards of the Self** are intrinsic — mastery, completion, control (finishing a game level, clearing an inbox).

He distinguishes **finite variability** (a mystery that runs out once solved, like a finished TV series) from **infinite variability** (content that keeps regenerating, like a social feed), which sustains interest far longer. Crucially, rewards must preserve the user's sense of **autonomy** — heavy-handedness triggers **reactance** — and must scratch the user's actual itch, not the company's.
readIf: Your product rewards users but engagement still flattens out
You want to know why feeds and loot boxes are so hard to put down
You're designing rewards and don't know which of the three types fits

chapters.title: Investment: The Work That Keeps Users
chapters.summary:
The final phase asks users to put something *in*, and that small investment is what pulls them back. Unlike the action phase (a quick behavior done before the reward), the **investment** comes after the reward and asks for a bit more effort — one that stores value and loads the next trigger.

Investments deposit **stored value** in five forms: **content, data, followers, reputation, and skill**. A playlist, a filled-out profile, a network of contacts, seller ratings, learned expertise — each makes the product better with use and harder to leave.

We over-value what we build. The **IKEA effect** (Ariely's research: labor breeds love) means users prize a service they've poured effort into. We also stay **consistent** with past behavior and resolve **cognitive dissonance** by adjusting attitudes to match our actions.

The mechanical payoff is that investments **load the next trigger**: following someone means their posts appear later; inviting a friend sets up a future notification. Each loop raises **switching costs** and stacks value, turning **escalating commitment** into a retention engine that compounds over time.
readIf: Users try your product once and never form a lasting tie to it
You want retention that compounds instead of leaking every month
You wonder why you can't quit a service you've poured years into

chapters.title: The Morality of Manipulation
chapters.summary:
The same techniques that build healthy habits can build harmful ones, so Eyal offers a tool for deciding which you're making: the **Manipulation Matrix**. It asks two questions — *Does the product materially improve the user's life?* and *Would the maker use it themselves?* — producing four roles.

The **Facilitator** builds something they'd use that also helps users; this is the ethical sweet spot and the best odds of success. The **Entertainer** builds something they'd use but that doesn't materially improve lives — permissible, though often fleeting. The **Peddler** believes their product helps others but wouldn't use it themselves, a gap that usually signals a weak product and shaky ethics. The **Dealer** builds something that neither helps users nor appeals to the maker — pure exploitation, and indefensible.

The simple gut-check is the **would-you-use-it test**: aim to be a Facilitator. Eyal argues builders bear real responsibility, since these methods can be aimed at health and learning or at addiction and waste, and users deserve makers who choose the former.
readIf: You're uneasy about whether what you're building is ethical
You want a concrete test, not a vague feeling, about manipulation
You want to judge whether an app is serving you or using you

chapters.title: Testing and Finding Hook Opportunities
chapters.summary:
A model is only useful if you can test it, so Eyal closes with method. The **Bible App** (YouVersion), built by Bobby Gruenewald, became a daily habit for tens of millions through daily-verse triggers, notifications, and personalization — proof the Hook Model works far outside Silicon Valley.

To tell real habits from wishful metrics, run **Habit Testing** in three steps. **Identify**: use cohort data to define who your devoted users are and how often a committed user *should* engage. **Codify**: find the **habit path** — the sequence of actions your loyal users share (Twitter's early insight that following 30 people was the tipping point is the classic example of this kind of discovery). **Modify**: change the product to steer new users down that path, then test again.

To find fresh opportunities, watch three places: **nascent behaviors** (things few people do yet, as early photo-sharing was), **enabling technologies** (new tech that makes the hook cycle faster or more rewarding), and **interface changes** (shifts in how people interact that put habits up for grabs).
readIf: You can't tell whether your users have a habit or just curiosity
You want a repeatable way to test and improve engagement
You're hunting for the next product opportunity before it's obvious
